Out in front, and by a big margin, are the trailbreakers copper and tin. With stocks-to-consumption ratios for these metals forecast to fall to record lows, set against a market with newfound confidence, we believe that prices will rise much further still, with copper prices expected to average more than $13,000/t by the end of the year. COMMODITIES RESEARCH 11 February 2011 PLEASE SEE ANALYST CERTIFICATIONS AND IMPORTANT DISCLOSURES STARTING AFTER PAGE 71 METALS MAGNIFIER A road less travelled  In a fashion that would have an explorer green with envy, the metals are entering unchartered territory this year. Already, there have been a series of firsts, with new landmarks being set along the way. Copper has broken through $10,000/t, tin is well above $30,000/t, palladium is challenging ten-year highs, while aluminium and gold physical premiums are scaling record and multi-year highs, respectively. Also, importantly, the market has a newfound confidence in global economic growth. The perception that a wave of speculative money is the driving force behind the run-up in base metal prices is misplaced, in our view. Data show that some investment vehicles have in fact had outflows (such as physically backed ETFs), while CTFC and LME open interest data show that there is not a significant overhang of long positions. However, there is appetite from all segments of the industry to participate in the metals space this year.

a significant overhang of long positions. However, there is appetite from all segments of the industry to participate in the metals space this year.  We are now at a crossroads, with the market in anticipation of a big pickup in buying and buyers in hope of lower prices; something will have to give. In particular, we will be watching for the quality of physical market indicators, not least LME stock trends and those relevant to the Chinese market, to improve. For now at least, the market appears to have come to terms with weakness in China’s post-holiday import buying, although we still view this as a short-term downside risk to prices. The road ahead is one less travelled, which means that price volatility is likely to remain elevated. But something is brewing, and we believe it is the beginning of a big increase in demand, which, together with a newfound confidence in the sustainability of economic growth, leads us to believe the route being mapped out is one of higher prices. For metals with positive fundamentals and whose market balances continue to tighten, we have revised our price forecasts higher to reflect this.

Barclays Capital | Metals Magnifier 11 February 2011 3 FOCUS Almost irreplaceable The run-up in copper prices to record highs has sparked discussion about the demand erosion that could be caused by substitution. Although there is evidence from the past that steep increases in prices can result in substitution, in our view concerns about the magnitude of potential demand losses for this year are excessive. Raw material decision making depends on a lot more than just price: factors such as price volatility, technological inferiority/superiority, competing product designs, regulatory changes and manufacturing location all contribute to the decision making process. That said, with copper’s main competitor, aluminium, trading at a quarter of copper’s level, the incentive to substitute is significant – although so far, consumer reports suggest that prices may have to rise much higher to spark any meaningful substitution. The primary market concern is the potential demand loss that could occur as a result of substitution.

ch higher to spark any meaningful substitution. The primary market concern is the potential demand loss that could occur as a result of substitution. In this month’s focus piece, we take top-down and bottom-up approaches to analyse this topic, beginning with a sensitivity analysis of consumption, prices and economic growth, before getting more granular and delving into specific product areas in which substitution can and is taking place and identifying areas of high risk. Finally, to provide a well-rounded view, we have also included analysis of new end-use areas for copper, a part of the picture that is often ignored, which can result in unbalanced views. In all, our analysis shows there are a number of hurdles to substitution and that in reality there are only a few areas that pose real potential for demand losses, albeit over time. Given these hurdles, we believe that the effect of substitution on copper demand over the next few years will be to slow the pace of potential demand growth slightly. For instance, based on our forecast for the copper/aluminium price ratio to rise much further this year, our analysis suggests that demand growth could be dented by a mere 0.57%.

Over the years, copper has lost market share in a variety of product areas: some price-related and some due to technological advancements. For instance, in the automotive market in the mid-1960s and 1970s (when the copper/aluminium ratio spiked), there was a large-scale move out of copper and into aluminium in the radiator market, and the vast majority of auto radiators are now aluminium. Copper has also been engineered out of the telecommunications market and replaced by fibre optics, which were deemed superior in performance. Barclays Capital | Metals Magnifier 11 February 2011 4 In contrast to other commodity sectors, such as oil and agriculture, the consumer tends not to have direct exposure to, or transparency of, the effect of the copper price on an end product. For instance, in the case of oil, the consumer has direct exposure to the price at the pump and, in agriculture, the price of the foodstuff on the supermarket shelf. In the copper market, however, price sensitivity lies with the manufacturer, which bears the cost directly and has to compete with lower cost competitors, rather than the consumer since copper usually comprises only a small proportion of the overall cost of a product. In an environment of prices hitting record highs and expectations for prices to remain elevated for a prolonged period, manufacturers will step up efforts to reduce procurement and manufacturing costs. However, changing raw material usage is not a flippant decision for manufacturers because there are costs associated and hurdles to overcome. Long-held standards and practices and ongoing relationships between suppliers and specifiers are slow to change.

associated and hurdles to overcome. Long-held standards and practices and ongoing relationships between suppliers and specifiers are slow to change. The high cost of retooling and retraining employees can discourage manufacturers from switching to other materials. Furthermore, even if a competing commodity is trading at a lower price, the eventual price advantage may not be as great as initially perceived. For instance, in the wire and cable market, the larger size of an aluminium or copper-clad aluminium cable compared with a pure copper cable will need more insulation, shielding, armour and may be more complex to fabricate, all of which may offset the initial cost saving. For this reason, many end-users often look for a copper/aluminium price ratio of 3 or 4. A macro perspective of substitution: Demand, GDP and price We have decided to tackle the issue of substitution using two methods. The first is a top-down approach that looks at the bigger macro picture and the relationship (or lack thereof) between copper prices, GDP and demand. By looking at the long-term relationship between copper consumption and GDP, we can see how this relationship has evolved.

er prices, GDP and demand. By looking at the long-term relationship between copper consumption and GDP, we can see how this relationship has evolved. Our analysis suggests copper demand has become less responsive to changes in global GDP. In the 1980s and 1990s, a 1% increase in global GDP would lead to up to a 2.5% increase in copper consumption. That relationship has weakened considerably, as over the past five years, a 1% increase in global GDP led to less than a 1% increase in global copper consumption. It would be dangerous to assume, however, that this weakening relationship was a pure reflection of the effects of substitution. Bigger, broader trends are predominantly at play here. Barclays Capital | Metals Magnifier 11 February 2011 5 copper demand growth. Between 2000 and 2009, the share of China, Brazil, India and the Middle East in global copper demand grew from 17% to 44%. China’s share of global copper demand at 38% is now almost twice that of the US. In measurements of GDP, emerging markets have a smaller weighting, though this is changing. This shift in the location of marginal demand is also having an important effect on the global intensity of copper use. This slowed substantially in the past decade, but is now beginning to rise as emerging markets account for an ever-growing share of global growth and is centred in the highly metals-intensive industrial development phase. Barclays Capital | Metals Magnifier 11 February 2011 6 ratio. Evidence suggests that between 1970 and 2010, the relationship between the two is not as evident as that between consumption and GDP. It also shows that big increases in the copper/aluminium price ratio have not materially depressed copper demand growth. In particular, the late 1980s and early 1990s saw a big rally in the copper/aluminium price ratio, which almost doubled in the space of five years. However, that was not followed by a sharp slowdown in copper demand. The same happened from 2001 onwards, when the copper/aluminium price ratio rose three-fold. Indeed, that period since 2001 has seen some of the largest increases in copper demand growth (the 9% in 2004 was the largest increase since 1976). Our analysis suggests that between 2003 and 2010 (when arguably substitution stepped up a gear due to steep increased in copper prices), an increase of 0.1 in the copper/aluminium ratio led to only a 0.05% decrease in copper demand. Using the 2003-2010 coefficient, which we believe is most reflective of current market structure, and applying that to our copper/aluminium price ratio of 4.6 for 2011 suggests demand growth would be dented by a mere 0.57%, all other things remaining equal. Construction The construction sector represents the source of greatest demand for copper, accounting for close to a third of total global consumption. Copper is used in a variety of areas such as building wire, plumbing and heating, as well as for architectural uses such as roofing. Despite construction being the area of largest use, the outlook for substitution risk here is limited, in our view, either because threats face significant qualitative hurdles or substitution effects are already close to completion because the case for the substitute has been persuasive for a long period of time. Perhaps the best example of the limited effect due to qualitative factors is in terms of building wire.

as been persuasive for a long period of time. Perhaps the best example of the limited effect due to qualitative factors is in terms of building wire. In the case of household wiring, the issue with using aluminium, the obvious substitute from a cost basis, has been with the reliability of connectors. Despite attempts in the US in the 1960-70s to push for wholesale substitution towards aluminium building wire – indeed penetration resulted in copper reaching its lowest market share at 71.9% of the total insulated wire and cable market in 1974 – the shift was ultimately stymied by safety problems with its application. In the case of residential wiring, problems with aluminium connectors (typically referred to as EC aluminium) resulted in a series of fires (which the US Consumer Product Safety Commission highlighted). As a result, the industry standard since has been copper. This is not limited to the US: in other countries such as India, where aluminium building wire achieved deep penetration in the past, this trend has been fully reversed due to the same perception of low safety. Will this move back to copper wire ever be reversed?

mid- 2000s, substitution was particularly aggressive – in 2005 and 2006, 7% and 8%, respectively, of the copper plumbing market was lost to plastics. In our view, this was the period when the so-called “easy-wins” were completed. Any substitution effects are unlikely in the short term, given that building regulation and codes would have to be amended, the sustained negative impressions of using alternatives and standard accepted procedures can be difficult to change. The second key area for consideration in construction is the plumbing sector. This is a case in which wholesale substitution towards plastic alternatives has been under way for a significant period. Plastic pipes and fittings are far less costly than copper, lighter and easier to install, as well as less liable to theft. While copper generally lasts longer and is more durable, plastics have now fully penetrated the piping market, and the majority of waste pipes carrying water away from sink drains and toilets are made of plastic. Copper still holds a key role in water distribution, although that could easily change in the future. In the mid- 2000s, substitution was particularly aggressive – in 2005 and 2006, 7% and 8%, respectively, of the copper plumbing market was lost to plastics.

Barclays Capital | Metals Magnifier 11 February 2011 7 substitution effects have slowed significantly – in 2009, there was only 41Kt of substitution from copper in the plumbing tube sector (out of a total of 394Kt). As a result, the sector has moved from the most important area of substitution in the mid-2000s to now being of mid- table significance. The final area of construction substitution has been in terms of roofing. This area has been under threat due to cost and theft for some time. Substitutes such as aluminium panels, galvanized steel or zinc have all offered as effective yet somewhat less aesthetically pleasing alternatives. However, as with the plumbing sector, we believe substitution rates have somewhat passed their peak, from 15% of total copper market substitution effects in 2006 to 4% by 2009 (ICA), and are unlikely to increase in significance in the short term. Transportation The transportation market accounts for 13% of global copper consumption (2.8Mt), with copper used in almost every vehicle from family cars to heavy trucks, locomotives and aircraft using copper in wiring, cabling, motors, radiators and busbars.

ed in almost every vehicle from family cars to heavy trucks, locomotives and aircraft using copper in wiring, cabling, motors, radiators and busbars. About 2% (9,000lbs, 4t) of the total weight of a Boeing 747-200 is copper, while a typical diesel-electric locomotive uses about 11,000 lbs (5t) and a typical US passenger car will use about 55- 70lbs (28Kg). In the transport sector, in particular for cars and aircraft, light-weighting is a key priority, so raw material decision making is not based purely on cost. Raw material usage patterns in the transportation sector can be more fluid than some other sectors due to the more regular and intensive research and development (R&D) undertaken by transportation companies, making this area potentially more open to substitution. One application in the transportation industry, where copper has been almost entirely substituted out of, is radiators. The trend first began in the mid-1960s when there was a large spike in the copper/aluminium price ratio (Figure 5). There was concerted development to engineer copper out of the radiators that was highly successful.

The areas of copper usage in the transportation market that we identify as being most at risk from substitution are battery cables and wire harnessing. Most automotive cables use copper conductors, though there have been some developments to use battery cables and wire harnessing with aluminium conductors. In the past, this was driven mainly by a need to lower weight, rather than reduce cost, though clearly in a market where copper is trading at four times the price of aluminium this will increase aluminium’s attractiveness as an Figure 6: Copper first-use consumption 2010 Figure 7: Net copper substitution losses by product in 2009 Wire 54% Plate, sheet & strip Alloys 19% Rods, bars & sections 3% Tube 11% Other 5% Plumbing tube 10% Commercial tube 14% Telecom cable 19% Non- insulated wirerod 8% Electrical/ electronic strip 13% Other 23% Alloy rod & bar 13% Source: Brook Hunt, Barclays Capital Source: International Copper Association, Barclays Capital Copper has been largely substituted out of the automotive radiator market High risk

The weight saving has become a particular focus in recent years because weight distribution of a high performance car is improved by placing the battery at the rear of the vehicle, requiring a battery cable several metres long, which would be very heavy if made of copper. Because of their physical size, battery cables with aluminium conductors may need to be made flat rather than circular and mounted underneath the vehicle. As a result of the more sophisticated connectors necessary for highly reliable connections, aluminium cable systems are not necessarily cheaper than copper equivalents, even though the conductor cost is much lower. This is an area in which there is ongoing R&D with the aim of improving the technology to make more reliable aluminium connectors. This is also an area in which CCA cable and wiring is being tested. Another area in which aluminium has begun to make inroads, albeit limited so far, is in busbars used in locomotives. These are effectively large distribution boards that can be either copper or aluminium.

ds, albeit limited so far, is in busbars used in locomotives. These are effectively large distribution boards that can be either copper or aluminium. Although we view this as a high risk area for copper substitution, it accounts for only a small portion of copper consumption in the transportation market. Copper’s use in catenary overhead cables is unlikely to face significant substitution threats, in our view, given the need for maximum conductivity and electrical power. Each kilometre of this cable uses 10t of copper. The focus to continually improve and meet changing customer preferences makes this a more dynamic end-use sector than say power cables, which is comparatively slow moving, particularly in developed markets. For these reasons, the speed at which substitution can take place in transportation is more rapid than some other sectors. However, the caveat is that it can take many years between the decision to change the product and it actually affecting buying patterns. This is generally the case for large-scale material changes in raw material usage. In other, smaller parts changes can be made more rapidly, especially when there are established routes already in place.

Barclays Capital | Metals Magnifier 11 February 2011 9 Within this sector, we believe there is one of the highest risk areas for ongoing substantial substitution: the heating ventilation and air-conditioning sector (HVAC), particularly in transportation. This is the area in which we expect the most rapid substitution. Aluminium has already made significant inroads into this sector, though we believe there is still potential for further, potentially sizeable, substitution. The quickest and most easily executed design change is to substitute round aluminium tube for round copper tube. The all-aluminium heat exchanger is also available and is the subject of continued development to improve design and technical competitiveness. In the developed European and US markets, aluminium has started to make inroads, with the route already set out by the automotive market, which has been using aluminium radiators for decades. As with many instances of substitution though, it is the developing markets that are using aluminium in this sector on a growing scale. We have heard anecdotal reports of substantial broad scale trials to shift to aluminium in Chinese air-conditioning manufacturing.

n a growing scale. We have heard anecdotal reports of substantial broad scale trials to shift to aluminium in Chinese air-conditioning manufacturing. This has been further evidenced by Brook Hunt reporting that Media, China’s largest air-conditioning manufacturer, has recently undertaken a trial to use aluminium alloy instead of copper tube for micro channel heat exchangers. Industrial machinery Copper is used in a variety of industrial machinery types such as in-plant equipment, industrial valves and fittings, non-electrical instruments, off-highway vehicles and heat exchangers. This represented close to 13% (2.8Mt) of global demand in 2009. Apart from the risks of substitution in heat exchangers, which are sizeable, there is some evidence of substitution in terms of industrial vales and fittings, as copper brass rod has suffered from miniaturisation and lower copper alloys. Gears and bearings have experienced minimal substitution, but there is greater potential for substitution towards aluminium composites in turbine blades. That said, there is little evidence to suggest this is a key area for potential shifts.

Electric and electronic products Power utilities and lighting & wiring devices Electronics and electrical applications consume 6Mt of copper (the second largest end-use sector), accounting for 28% of demand. Within that sector, competing materials have made inroads in the power cable market, but only to a limited degree given the necessary technical specifications, such as conductivity. However, preferences vary across the world, with Indian and Brazilian power utilities preferring aluminium conductors to copper in insulated power cables. Given the need for national standardisation, it is more challenging to change raw material usage in the power cable market. A key problem in this area is that it is more difficult to link an aluminium cable with a copper network and connections. For this reason, industrial users of power cables are more likely than utilities to prefer copper conductors as contractors may not have the specialist skills needed for installing cables with aluminium conductors.

than utilities to prefer copper conductors as contractors may not have the specialist skills needed for installing cables with aluminium conductors. There is a need for standardisation that is a limiting factor in changes in raw material usage. Although there is plenty of potential for substitution in power utilities, this sector is generally slow in making technical changes. Favoured cable designs once established will be retained for a long time. The market is generally divided into three categories: low-voltage (LV), medium-voltage (MV) and high-voltage (HV).  Low voltage – used for connection between a residential or small commercial customer and the utility. In the LV market, copper clad aluminium cables (CCA) have made inroads, especially in the emerging markets such as China and India, with Chinese Heat exchangers are a high risk sector for copper substitution The need for standardisation makes changes in raw material use very slow moving

Copper wires are commonly used in MV distribution and LV connections to premises, though in newer markets aluminium is making inroads.  Medium voltage (distribution) – used for distribution in urban and rural areas. Copper wires are commonly used in MV distribution to premises, though in newer markets aluminium is making inroads.  High voltage (subtransmission and transmission) – used for transmission of bulk quantities of electric power and connection to very large consumers. The bare wire conductors on the line are generally made of aluminium (either plain or reinforced with steel or sometimes composite materials). Aluminium conductors reinforced with steel (known as ACSR) are primarily used for MV and HV lines and may also be used for overhead services to individual customers. Aluminium conductors are used because it has better resistivity/weight than copper, as well as being cheaper, requiring less supports and being more wind resistant.

ctors are used because it has better resistivity/weight than copper, as well as being cheaper, requiring less supports and being more wind resistant. Copper clad aluminium (CCA) wire and cable has grown in popularity over the past few years, in particular in the emerging markets, which have huge infrastructure build needs. In developed markets, CCA wiring is more commonly used in the form of enamelled magnet wire with inroads increasingly being made in applications such as headphones and aircraft instruments (where weight saving is a priority). In coaxial cables, the high frequency signal is transmitted in the surface layers, and therefore the aluminium (or in some cases steel) wire core does not detract from the absolute performance. But, as with many of the changes in raw material usage, emerging markets are leading the way in large part because there are generally fewer or no standard accepted practices, making it easier to implement new or different technologies. CCA consists of copper cladding surrounding an aluminium core, or in the case of CAA wiring, the aluminium wires are copper coated.

ent technologies. CCA consists of copper cladding surrounding an aluminium core, or in the case of CAA wiring, the aluminium wires are copper coated. The most common grade of CCA contains 10% copper by volume and has 65% of the conductivity of an equivalent all-copper cable. Given its inferior conductivity, CCA is only used in certain applications. A key consideration is that CCA cable has a much wider diameter than a pure copper cable. This characteristic, together with its comparatively light weight, means they are more commonly used in overhead applications than underground. However, one of the main drawbacks of using aluminium wires and cables is that they need specially designed accessories to give reliable electrical connections. Cable-specialist knowledge is required, making wide-scale implementation difficult. Another factor that has restricted more penetration of CAA wiring and cable in developed markets is a limited number of suppliers. However, the massive growth in production of this product in China means it has become more widely available in recent years.

ed number of suppliers. However, the massive growth in production of this product in China means it has become more widely available in recent years. Cost is clearly the key consideration when choosing a CCA cable, yet the savings are not as straightforward as the difference between the copper and aluminium prices. A CCA cable is more complicated to fabricate and so has higher fabrication costs, as well as higher auxiliary input costs due to its larger size than a pure copper cable. That said, these additional costs are more than offset in a market in which copper is trading at four times the cost of aluminium. Although the scope for potential substitution in the power cable and wiring devices markets is large, we view this as only a medium-risk area given the challenges faced in changing component parts in this area. Aluminium’s inferior conductivity is a significant drawback, while the need for standardisation and considerations of size add further hurdles to using materials other than copper. Substitution in this area tends to be slower moving than other sectors, so we expect net losses in consumption to be marginal over the next two years.

Barclays Capital | Metals Magnifier 11 February 2011 11 Telecommunication cable Along with plumbing in the building and construction sector, copper usage in telecommunications has declined significantly due to substitution over the past decade. While cost inevitably played a part, technical performance was the main reason. Fibre optic cables have largely displaced copper telecom cables in a number of applications, such as trunk telecom networks, because they give a superior performance with higher productivity. So far it has largely involved long distance trunk lines, while the local connection has until recently remained copper. With well-established routes for substitution, together with the superior technical performance of competing materials such as fibre optics, we believe further substitution in this sector could be substantial. However, given that copper has already lost a lot of market share, the net effect on tonnage consumed is likely to be small. Electronics Unlike the other sectors we have discussed, electronic is the only sector in which there has been a broad shift from aluminium towards copper.

ronics Unlike the other sectors we have discussed, electronic is the only sector in which there has been a broad shift from aluminium towards copper. Major electronic manufacturers have increasingly been engineering copper into their products in recent years as aluminium had previously been used due to shifting technical demands. The need for smaller, faster and more energy efficient components is increasingly rendering aluminium as an inferior raw material. Aluminium’s low mechanical strength means it can crack when it becomes stressed, which is in direct conflict with the increasing volumes of stress semiconductors are subjected to. This problem has been exacerbated by the demand for semiconductors to become smaller in size. For these reasons, copper is increasingly favoured as a superior raw material for semiconductors because it has higher mechanical strength, is more ductile and has superior electrical conductivity. The result is faster operating speeds and greater circuit integration. Given the changing technical demand of semiconductors, which has already been moving in favour of using copper rather than aluminium, we view the risks of substitution in this sector as small.

First, the move towards greener, more energy-efficient economies is a policy shift that stands to benefit copper, given its quality as an excellent conductor and heat transferor. Certainly, while copper may lose out to aluminium in terms of weight, electric and hybrid vehicles still use two to three times more copper than in standard equivalent-sized vehicles. For example, the new electric vehicles from Ford Motor Company, including their hybrid electric, battery electric (that are all electric) and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (run on a battery plus gas engine), use more copper for all of their high and low voltage wiring and in the motor winding than in your average ‘non electric’ vehicle. Indeed, more than half of the 68-82Kg of copper used in electric cars is in the wiring harness and electrical components. The inverter system (to deliver power to the motors) alone uses 8-18Kg. The additional copper is needed because the motor in electric vehicles is much larger and the wiring needs to carry a higher current and voltage.

Within the green theme, new sources of renewable power also are well leveraged to copper’s energy efficiency. For example, in wind turbines, copper used in the grounding of the towers in the event of lightning transports electricity to substations and connects the system into the utility grid. Given that the turbines are often located far from substations, the length of copper wiring and cabling needed is massive. This is particularly the case for offshore wind turbines, which require huge lengths of undersea cabling. Copper also plays a critical role in the turbine motors and their control wires. Onshore wind farms can require 6t of copper per MW of generating capacity, or a total of 16t per turbine. Split by usage, 5t is contained in the turbine and its transformer, while 11t is contained in the cabling. Offshore wind farms require even greater volumes of copper with 31t of copper per turbine, which is 10t per MW.

Spreads have also continued to tighten, with the LME cash-to-3-month contango halving over the course of January. Moreover, for the first time in close to six months, the nickel market had a net draw in LME stocks over a calendar month period (albeit a modest 3Kt), which ties in with our expectation that Q1 11 will see a deficit dynamic re-emerge after a softer period for fundamentals in H2 10. We expect these draws to build in pace and upward momentum in prices to continue in February with $30,000/t a near term target.

undamentals in H2 10. We expect these draws to build in pace and upward momentum in prices to continue in February with $30,000/t a near term target.  A combination of factors are supporting this tightening in fundamentals. First, in line with robust economic data, demand from the stainless sector has picked up across key regions. In China, anecdotal reports suggest strong demand coming back from the stainless sector, which is in line with positive trade data in Q4 and the strengthening in price spread. This adjoins robust activity in Europe and the US, where restocking and end-demand improvements supported the market. Second, the supply side continues to offer clear constraints on production growth. The effect of rising coking coal prices on NPI in China and the high disruption risk attached to high- pressure acid leach operations are already obvious. In addition though, January has seen QNI’s 30Kty Yabulu refinery in Queensland affected by adverse weather, as well as minor disruptions at Vale’s Sudbury and Voisey’s Bay facilities. All this combines to make the 2011 deficit ever more significant and price supportive, as such we have revised higher our price deck for 2011.
